theorem_title: Khatri–Rao Gram identity / CP-HIFI-style local algebraic lemma
theorem_statement:
Let \(d\ge 2\), let \(k\in \{1,\ldots,d\}\), and for each mode \(j\ne k\), let

\[
A^{(j)}\in \mathbb R^{n_j\times r}
\]

be a real matrix. For a multi-index

\[
\alpha=(\alpha_j)_{j\ne k}\in \prod_{j\ne k}\{1,\ldots,n_j\},
\]

define a row vector \(b_\alpha\in\mathbb R^r\) by

\[
b_\alpha(s)=\prod_{j\ne k} A^{(j)}_{\alpha_j,s}.
\]

Let \(B\) be the matrix whose rows are the \(b_\alpha\), indexed by all such multi-indices \(\alpha\). Thus

\[
B_{\alpha,s}=\prod_{j\ne k} A^{(j)}_{\alpha_j,s}.
\]

For each \(j\ne k\), define the Gram matrix

\[
G^{(j)}=(A^{(j)})^T A^{(j)},
\]

so

\[
G^{(j)}_{s,t}=\sum_{a=1}^{n_j}A^{(j)}_{a,s}A^{(j)}_{a,t}.
\]

Then

\[
B^T B
=

\bigcirc_{j\ne k} G^{(j)},
\]

where \(\bigcirc\) denotes entrywise, or Hadamard, product. Equivalently, for every \(s,t\in \{1,\ldots,r\}\),

\[
(B^T B)_{s,t}
=

\prod_{j\ne k} G^{(j)}_{s,t}.
\]
raw_proof_text:
Fix two column indices \(s,t\in \{1,\ldots,r\}\). By definition of matrix multiplication,

\[
(B^T B)_{s,t}
=

\sum_{\alpha} B_{\alpha,s}B_{\alpha,t},
\]

where the sum is over all multi-indices

\[
\alpha=(\alpha_j)_{j\ne k}\in \prod_{j\ne k}\{1,\ldots,n_j\}.
\]

Using the definition of \(B\),

\[
B_{\alpha,s}
=

\prod_{j\ne k} A^{(j)}_{\alpha_j,s},
\qquad
B_{\alpha,t}
=

\prod_{j\ne k} A^{(j)}_{\alpha_j,t}.
\]

Therefore

\[
B_{\alpha,s}B_{\alpha,t}
=

\prod_{j\ne k}
\left(
A^{(j)}_{\alpha_j,s}A^{(j)}_{\alpha_j,t}
\right).
\]

So

\[
(B^T B)_{s,t}
=

\sum_{\alpha\in\prod_{j\ne k}\{1,\ldots,n_j\}}
\prod_{j\ne k}
\left(
A^{(j)}_{\alpha_j,s}A^{(j)}_{\alpha_j,t}
\right).
\]

Now use the elementary finite-sum factorization identity:

\[
\sum_{\alpha\in\prod_{j\ne k} I_j}
\prod_{j\ne k} h_j(\alpha_j)
=

\prod_{j\ne k}
\sum_{a\in I_j} h_j(a).
\]

In our case,

\[
I_j=\{1,\ldots,n_j\},
\qquad
h_j(a)=A^{(j)}_{a,s}A^{(j)}_{a,t}.
\]

Thus

\[
(B^T B)_{s,t}
=

\prod_{j\ne k}
\left(
\sum_{a=1}^{n_j}
A^{(j)}_{a,s}A^{(j)}_{a,t}
\right).
\]

But the term in parentheses is exactly

\[
G^{(j)}_{s,t}.
\]

Therefore

\[
(B^T B)_{s,t}
=

\prod_{j\ne k}G^{(j)}_{s,t}.
\]

Since this holds for every pair of column indices \(s,t\), we have

\[
B^T B
=

\bigcirc_{j\ne k}G^{(j)}.
\]

This proves the theorem.
